And as a byproduct of everybody sort of pursuing their individual self -interest and trying to persuade each other and justify themselves, sometimes what you get is an emergent outcome which is good for the group. fired up and focused on the particular reasons for that view, and then you get a bunch, you basically, you get a bunch of different people working on justifications for different views, and the views that rise to the top that succeed, that prevail, tend to be the ones that are more well justified, well reasoned. Is that basically the claim you're making? Yeah, exactly. And that's because...
On the one hand, so when you're producing arguments, when you're producing reasons, when you're reasoning in a more active manner, indeed your goal is to either, when you're doing it live, kind of just to persuade people to change their minds either about the conclusion you're trying to defend or at least about what they think of you when you're justifying yourself, you don't, you know, you want them to think that your choices and your beliefs are rational.
But there's also the more kind of passive position, which is when you're evaluating other people's reasons and arguments and justifications, and then your goal is much more objective. The goal is really to figure out are these good reasons and should I change my mind on the basis of these reasons. And the two of them are really completely necessary for a group, for both for individuals, because if my goal was to convince others, but they would never change their minds, then obviously it would be pointless.
